children.name: Pica
children.description: The true magpies — unmistakable black-and-white birds with an extraordinarily long, iridescent green tail. Among the most intelligent animals on Earth, magpies pass the mirror self-recognition test, use tools, hold 'funerals' for dead companions, and cache food while watching for observers, then move the cache if they suspect they have been seen. The Eurasian Magpie has lived alongside humans for millennia and thrives in cities, gardens, and farmland across the entire Palearctic.

children.name: Pica hudsonia
children.description: The North American counterpart of the Eurasian Magpie, found from Alaska to the Great Plains and western mountain ranges. It builds enormous domed nests of sticks lined with mud, which may be reused or appropriated by other birds for years. Highly adaptable, it forages alongside livestock and bison, picking ticks from their hides.

children.name: Pica nuttalli
children.description: Endemic to California's Central Valley and surrounding foothills, this is the only corvid restricted to a single US state. Its vivid yellow bill and orbital skin distinguish it from its black-billed relative. Social and gregarious, it nests in loose colonies in valley oaks and is vulnerable to West Nile virus, which caused major population declines in the 2000s.

children.name: Pica mauritanica
children.description: Restricted to the coastal forests and woodland edges of Algeria, Morocco, and Tunisia, this recently recognised species was long treated as a subspecies of the Eurasian Magpie. It differs in having a broader blue orbital ring and slightly smaller body size. It inhabits olive groves, orchards, and scrubby hillsides in the Mediterranean zone of North Africa.

children.name: Pica asirensis
children.description: Found only in the Asir Mountains of south-western Saudi Arabia, this isolate population was elevated to full species status based on differences in morphology and genetics. It inhabits juniper and acacia woodland on mountain escarpments above 1,500 m. Its restricted range and small population make it one of the most geographically limited corvids in the world.

children.name: Corvus
children.description: Crows and ravens — the largest and most wide-ranging genus in the family, with an almost global distribution. Corvus species are renowned for problem-solving, social complexity, and long memory for individual human faces. The Common Raven is the largest passerine and one of the most intelligent birds known, capable of planning, deception, and playful behaviour.

children.name: Corvus brachyrhynchos
children.description: One of the most familiar birds in North America, the American Crow inhabits virtually every habitat from forest to desert to urban centre. It has a complex social structure in which young birds from previous years help raise subsequent broods, and populations gather in winter roosts of millions of individuals. Crows remember and hold grudges against individual humans, and mobbing of perceived threats is coordinated across family groups.

children.name: Corvus ossifragus
children.description: A coastal and riparian crow of the eastern United States, closely tied to rivers, lakes, and seashores where it forages for fish, crabs, eggs, and refuse. Smaller and more nasal-voiced than the American Crow, the two species often flock together and can only be reliably told apart by call. It has been expanding inland along river systems as riparian tree cover has increased.

children.name: Corvus cryptoleucus
children.description: The smaller of the two North American ravens, this species is characteristic of the arid grasslands and desert scrub of the Chihuahuan Desert and adjacent regions. Its white feather bases (visible only when wind ruffles the plumage) give it the epithet cryptoleucus. It is far more gregarious than the Common Raven, forming flocks of hundreds and communal roosts of thousands in winter.

children.name: Corvus moneduloides
children.description: Famed as one of the world's most sophisticated tool users, this crow is endemic to New Caledonia and the Loyalty Islands and manufactures hooked tools from twigs and leaves of the Pandanus palm to probe for beetle larvae — a behaviour transmitted culturally between generations. Laboratory experiments have demonstrated planning, causal reasoning, and meta-cognition in this species, placing its cognitive abilities on par with great apes.

children.name: Corvus albus
children.description: The most widespread crow in sub-Saharan Africa, recognisable by its clean white breast and collar contrasting with glossy black upperparts. It is supremely adaptable and thrives in cities, villages, open savannah, and coastal areas alike. An opportunistic omnivore, it follows human settlement and has expanded its range as land clearance has created new open foraging habitat.

children.name: Corvus crassirostris
children.description: The largest member of the genus by body length, this impressive raven inhabits the highlands of Ethiopia and Eritrea from 1,500 to 4,500 m. Its massive, strongly arched bill with a white patch near the tip is unique among corvids and is used to prise open carcasses and break hard food items. It is a bold scavenger at abattoirs and rubbish tips in Addis Ababa, and plays an important cultural role in Ethiopian folklore.

children.name: Corvus capensis
children.description: A glossy all-black crow of open grasslands, semi-arid scrub, and farmland in eastern and southern Africa. It has an unusually slender, elongated bill compared with other crows, and a ringing, melodic call distinct from its relatives. It often follows tractors and ploughs to feed on exposed invertebrates, and pairs hold large year-round territories.

children.name: Corvus ruficollis
children.description: A desert raven of the Sahara, Arabian Peninsula, and arid zones from Morocco to Pakistan, characterised by a distinctive brown or purplish-brown wash on the neck and breast. Highly nomadic, it follows rainfall and temporary food sources across vast arid landscapes. Despite harsh conditions it is a year-round resident of some of the driest deserts on Earth, breeding on cliff ledges and palm trees.

children.name: Corvus rhipidurus
children.description: A stout, short-tailed raven of cliffs and gorges from the Horn of Africa through the Arabian Peninsula to Pakistan, recognised by its broad, fan-shaped tail and remarkably deep, resonant call. It is a masterful aerialist, riding thermals above desert escarpments with the ease of a vulture. It nests on cliff ledges alongside Egyptian Vultures and is often associated with rocky mountain ranges and ancient ruins.

children.name: Corvus coronoides
children.description: The largest corvid in Australia, distinguished by its distinctive wailing call — a long, drawn-out 'ahh' that descends in pitch — and the elongated hackles on the throat that are raised during calling. It inhabits woodland, forest, and farmland across south-eastern Australia and is a highly territorial bird that defends its home range year-round. Like other Australian corvids, it has notably white inner webs to its feathers.

children.name: Corvus bennetti
children.description: The smallest of the Australian corvids, this species inhabits the arid and semi-arid interior of Australia, particularly mulga and mallee scrubland. It is strongly nomadic, following rainfall and eruptions of grasshoppers and other invertebrates across vast distances. Gregarious outside the breeding season, it forms large flocks that wheel and tumble acrobatically over the outback in a distinctive display.

children.name: Corvus orru
children.description: The most widespread corvid in northern Australia, New Guinea, and the Moluccas, this adaptable species thrives in tropical woodland, forest edges, mangroves, and urban areas. It has an all-black plumage with white bases to the neck feathers — visible when the bird fans its hackles — and a harsh, abrupt call. It readily exploits human settlements and agricultural areas, and is the dominant crow of northern Australian cities.

children.name: Corvus typicus
children.description: Endemic to Sulawesi and nearby islands in Indonesia, this medium-sized crow is distinctive for its white belly and breast contrasting with glossy black head and upperparts. Its common name comes from its clear, piping calls, quite unlike the harsh notes of most crows. It inhabits forest, forest edges, and open woodland from lowlands to mountain zones, and is often seen in small noisy groups.

children.name: Corvus florensis
children.description: One of the world's rarest corvids, endemic to the island of Flores in the Lesser Sundas, Indonesia, where it is restricted to montane forest above 1,000 m. An all-black crow of small to medium size, it is poorly known and rarely observed. Deforestation has substantially reduced its habitat and it is considered vulnerable, with a population estimated at fewer than 3,500 individuals.

children.name: Corvus kubaryi
children.description: Critically endangered and confined to the islands of Guam and Rota in the Mariana Islands, this crow has been nearly eliminated from Guam by introduced brown tree snakes, with fewer than 60 individuals surviving there. The Rota population is more stable but threatened by habitat loss. Named after the Polish naturalist Jan Kubary, it is an important seed disperser in the limestone forests of the Marianas.

children.name: Coloeus
children.description: Jackdaws — compact, sociable corvids with pale eyes and a distinctive silver-grey nape. Among the most social corvids, they form long-term pair bonds, nest colonially, and regularly follow larger ungulates and humans to catch disturbed insects. The Eurasian Jackdaw's silver eye gives it an unusually expressive, inquisitive gaze.

children.name: Garrulus
children.description: The Eurasian Jays — dazzlingly patterned corvids with a sky-blue wing panel, pinkish-brown body, and a raucous, scolding call that serves as a woodland alarm system. Jays are paramount seed dispersers: a single bird buries thousands of acorns each autumn in separate caches up to several kilometres from the source tree, and though it recovers most, enough forgotten caches germinate to drive oak forest regeneration across Europe.

children.name: Cyanopica
children.description: Azure-winged magpies — slender, long-tailed corvids with pale underparts and delicate sky-blue wings and tail. Unusually social for corvids, they breed cooperatively with helpers at the nest. They have a striking disjunct distribution: the Iberian Magpie in Portugal and Spain, and the Azure-winged Magpie from the Russian Far East to Japan and China — a distribution once attributed to human introduction but now confirmed as natural.

children.name: Nucifraga
children.description: Nutcrackers — thickset corvids of mountain conifer forests, with a specialised sublingual pouch that can carry up to 90 pine seeds at once. The Spotted Nutcracker is the primary disperser of Swiss stone pine and Arolla pine across the Alps and Carpathians; without it, these trees could not colonise new ground above the existing treeline. The mutualism is so tight that the trees produce seeds too large for most birds other than nutcrackers to handle.

children.name: Nucifraga columbiana
children.description: An iconic bird of western North American mountain forests, Clark's Nutcracker stores up to 98,000 whitebark pine seeds per year in thousands of individual caches scattered across up to 35 km², and retrieves them throughout winter using extraordinary spatial memory. Unclaimed seeds germinate to regenerate whitebark pine forests above the treeline. The species is thus the primary agent of whitebark pine dispersal and a keystone species in subalpine ecosystems.

children.name: Pyrrhocorax
children.description: Choughs — sleek, all-black corvids with curved bills and buoyant, acrobatic flight that makes them the most aerial members of the family. They inhabit mountain cliffs, sea-cliffs, and upland pastures, probing the soil and dung for invertebrates with their specialised bills. Their high, ringing calls echo off cliff faces throughout their range from Ireland to China.

children.name: Perisoreus
children.description: Siberian jays — small, fluffy corvids of the taiga, with soft grey-brown plumage and a rusty tail. They are year-round residents of boreal forest and have developed an extraordinary relationship with human hunters, following them through the forest to scavenge scraps — a behaviour that has made them symbols of the northern wilderness in Scandinavian folklore. They cache food year-round and retrieve caches with remarkable spatial memory during winter.

children.name: Perisoreus canadensis
children.description: Known historically as the Gray Jay or Whisky Jack, this bold camp robber of the boreal forests of Canada and the northern United States is famous for stealing food from campsites and hunters. It begins nesting in late winter, sometimes with snow still on the ground, and stores food in caches of sticky saliva-coated pellets pressed into bark crevices — reserves that sustain it and its chicks through the coldest months.

children.name: Urocissa
children.description: Blue magpies — spectacular corvids of South and East Asian forests, with vivid cobalt-blue plumage, a white-tipped tail sometimes longer than the body, and a red or yellow bill. They are noisy, gregarious birds of forest edges and hillsides, often moving in small parties. Despite their dazzling appearance they behave much like other corvids — omnivorous, opportunistic, and bold.

children.name: Urocissa caerulea
children.description: Endemic to the mountains of Taiwan, the Taiwan Blue Magpie is one of the world's most spectacular corvids, with a deep cobalt-blue body, chestnut wings, a striking black-and-white long tail, and a vivid red bill and legs. In 2007 it was voted Taiwan's national bird in a public poll. It inhabits broadleaf mountain forest between 300 and 1,200 m and is notable for cooperative group defence of nests.

children.name: Urocissa ornata
children.description: Endemic to the wet-zone forests of Sri Lanka, this endangered blue magpie is smaller than its mainland relatives and has rich chestnut wings and a shorter tail. It inhabits dense primary and secondary forest in the hills of the island's south-western wet zone, where it forages in small groups through the understorey and mid-canopy. Habitat loss to tea estates and logging has severely reduced its range.

children.name: Urocissa whiteheadi
children.description: A distinctive blue magpie of hill forests in southern China, northern Vietnam, and Hainan Island, recognised by large white wing patches that flash conspicuously in flight. Named after the British ornithologist John Whitehead who collected specimens in Hainan in the 1890s. It inhabits montane broadleaf forests between 800 and 2,000 m, often in small groups of up to a dozen birds that move noisily through the canopy.

children.name: Dendrocitta
children.description: Treepies — long-tailed corvids of South and Southeast Asian woodlands with warm rufous and grey plumage. Unlike most corvids they are strongly arboreal, racing through canopy and clambering along branches in search of fruit, insects, eggs, and small vertebrates. Their loud, varied calls are a characteristic sound of the Indian subcontinent's forests and gardens.

children.name: Dendrocitta leucogastra
children.description: Endemic to the forests of the Western Ghats and associated hills of south India, the White-bellied Treepie is the most range-restricted member of its genus. It inhabits dense evergreen and semi-evergreen forest, typically in hilly terrain, and rarely descends to the ground. Its clean white belly contrasting with a rufous back and long tail makes it one of the more distinctive treepies.

children.name: Dendrocitta occipitalis
children.description: Found in the montane forests of Sumatra and Borneo, the Sunda Treepie inhabits hill and lower montane forest between 900 and 2,400 m. It has a distinctly greyish head and nape and a white patch on the wing, separating it from mainland relatives. Like other treepies it is a noisy canopy forager, using its strong bill to probe bark and tear open decaying wood in search of invertebrates.

children.name: Dendrocitta frontalis
children.description: A poorly known treepie of the eastern Himalayas and adjacent hills of Bangladesh, Myanmar, and southern China, identified by its narrow black collar on the lower throat. It inhabits humid broadleaf forest and secondary growth at elevations of 600 to 1,800 m. Its ecology is little studied but it is presumed to share the frugivore-omnivore lifestyle typical of treepies.

children.name: Dendrocitta cinerascens
children.description: Endemic to the mountains of Borneo, this treepie is found in montane forest above 900 m, reaching 3,000 m on Mount Kinabalu. It is largely grey with rufous undertail coverts and a long tail, and is often encountered in mixed-species foraging flocks moving through the subalpine oak forests. It is more insectivorous than lowland treepies and frequently joins flocks with babblers and laughingthrushes.

children.name: Dendrocitta bayleii
children.description: Endemic to the Andaman Islands in the Bay of Bengal, this near-threatened treepie inhabits tropical rainforest on the larger islands of the Andaman group. It is recognised by its entirely dark rufous-brown plumage and long graduated tail, and differs markedly from mainland treepies. Like other island corvids it tends to be tamer than continental relatives, making it vulnerable to hunting pressure.

children.name: Cissa
children.description: Green magpies — small, brilliantly coloured corvids of South and Southeast Asian forests, with vivid emerald-green plumage (which fades to turquoise or blue in captivity due to a diet-dependent carotenoid pigment), bright red bill and legs, and a chestnut wing panel. They are secretive inhabitants of forest understorey and mid-storey, where their coloration provides effective camouflage against sunlit foliage.

children.name: Cissa chinensis
children.description: The most widespread green magpie, found from the eastern Himalayas through Southeast Asia to Sumatra and Borneo. Its vivid green plumage, red bill, and chestnut wing panel make it unmistakable, though the green fades to blue when the bird is deprived of carotenoid-rich prey. An active predator of lizards, small frogs, and large insects, it is as much a hunter as a forager.

children.name: Cissa hypoleuca
children.description: Closely related to the Common Green Magpie but with a paler, more yellowish-green tone and white or pale buff underparts, this species occupies the lowland and foothill forests of Indochina from southern China to Vietnam, Laos, and Thailand. It was long treated as a subspecies of Cissa chinensis before molecular work confirmed its distinctness. It inhabits dense secondary forest and bamboo stands.

children.name: Cissa jefferyi
children.description: Endemic to the mountains of Borneo, where it inhabits montane forest above 900 m, this is the least well-known of the green magpies. It is distinguished from the Common Green Magpie by its higher-altitude range, slightly duller plumage, and subtle structural differences. Like its relatives it is a secretive forest bird that moves quietly through the understorey, more often heard than seen.

children.name: Cissa thalassina
children.description: Critically endangered and confined to a few remaining patches of submontane forest in western Java, this species has suffered catastrophic declines due to deforestation and trapping for the caged-bird trade, where its vivid plumage commands high prices. With a wild population estimated at fewer than 50 individuals, it is one of the most threatened corvids in the world and is the subject of captive breeding programmes in Indonesian zoos.

children.name: Crypsirina
children.description: Racket-tailed treepies — slender, long-tailed corvids of mainland Southeast Asia, with blackish or brownish plumage and a distinctive spatula-tipped tail formed by the elongated, bare-shafted central rectrices. They inhabit open woodland, scrub, and bamboo, and are more tolerant of disturbed habitats than most of their relatives.

children.name: Crypsirina temia
children.description: Found from Myanmar and Thailand south through the Malay Peninsula to Java and Bali, this distinctive treepie has glossy bluish-black plumage and two elongated central tail feathers tipped with rackets of soft feather webs. It inhabits open deciduous forest, bamboo, scrub, and the edges of cultivation, often in small parties. Its call is a variety of metallic clicks and churring notes.

children.name: Crypsirina cucullata
children.description: Near-endemic to Myanmar, where it inhabits dry deciduous forest and scrub in the central dry zone, with a small range extending into neighbouring parts of Thailand. Distinguished by its pale grey body contrasting with a black head and upper breast, and a long, slender tail. It is classified as vulnerable due to its small range, ongoing habitat degradation, and trapping for the caged-bird trade.

children.name: Ptilostomus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Piapiac, a slender, long-tailed African corvid with a social lifestyle more reminiscent of mynas than typical crows. The Piapiac is unique among corvids in its association with large mammals, riding on the backs of cattle, camels, and elephants to pick ticks, flies, and disturbed insects from their hides.

children.name: Ptilostomus afer
children.description: A highly social, black, long-tailed corvid of the savannah and open woodland from Senegal to Ethiopia and Uganda, the Piapiac is most often seen in noisy flocks following cattle, camels, or elephants and picking parasites from their skin. It nests colonially in palm trees, and groups defend nests cooperatively. Its pinkish-mauve bill base and reddish-brown eyes distinguish it from other African corvids.

children.name: Zavattariornis
children.description: A monotypic genus known only from a tiny area of southern Ethiopia, containing one of the world's most enigmatic corvids. Stresemann's Bush Crow was unknown to science until 1938 and remains poorly understood, with a bizarrely restricted range that appears to be determined by specific climatic conditions rather than vegetation type.

children.name: Zavattariornis stresemanni
children.description: A small, pale grey corvid of dry Acacia-Commiphora scrub around Yabello in the Borana region of southern Ethiopia, where its entire world population of an estimated 10,000 birds is confined to an area of roughly 6,000 km². Named after the German ornithologist Erwin Stresemann, this cooperative breeder is threatened by climate change, which appears to be shrinking the specific temperature envelope that determines its range limits.

children.name: Cyanocitta
children.description: North American blue jays — unmistakable corvids with brilliant blue, white, and black plumage and a conspicuous crest. Among the most familiar birds of North America, they are highly vocal, intelligent, and adaptable, thriving in forests, suburbs, and parks. They are important seed dispersers of oaks and other mast-producing trees, and bold mobbers of raptors and other predators.

children.name: Cyanocitta cristata
children.description: One of the most recognisable birds of eastern North America, the Blue Jay is famed for its intelligence, raucous voice, and striking blue-and-white plumage with a distinctive crest. It plays a vital ecological role as an acorn disperser: a single bird caches up to 5,000 acorns per autumn, leading to widespread oak regeneration. It is also a skilled mimic, often imitating Red-tailed Hawk calls to drive other birds from feeders.

children.name: Cyanocitta stelleri
children.description: The only crested jay west of the Rocky Mountains, Steller's Jay inhabits coniferous forests from Alaska to Nicaragua and is a dominant bird of western North American mountain woodlands. Named after the German naturalist Georg Wilhelm Steller who collected it in Alaska in 1741, it is a bold camp visitor and an excellent mimic, capable of reproducing the calls of Red-tailed and Red-shouldered Hawks. It is the most polytypic species in the genus with 16 recognised subspecies.

children.name: Aphelocoma
children.description: Scrub-jays — crrestless, blue-and-grey jays of North and Central America, inhabiting scrubby oak woodland, chaparral, and semi-arid habitats. They are notable for their exceptional episodic memory: Western Scrub-Jays remember what, where, and when they cached food, and take account of whether a rival was watching when they hid it — a cognitive feat that implies an awareness of other minds.

children.name: Aphelocoma californica
children.description: A bold, crestless jay of the chaparral, oak woodland, and suburban gardens of the California coast and foothills, split from the Western Scrub-Jay complex in 2016. It demonstrates remarkable episodic-like memory, recaching food when it suspects a rival has observed its cache location. One of the few corvids to regularly attend the bodies of dead conspecifics, gathering in 'funerals' reminiscent of those described in magpies.

children.name: Aphelocoma coerulescens
children.description: The only bird species endemic to Florida, the Florida Scrub-Jay is a cooperative breeder in which offspring from previous years help their parents raise subsequent broods. It is confined to the rare Florida scrub habitat — ancient sand-ridge communities dominated by sand pines and scrub oaks — and is threatened by fire suppression, which allows its open habitat to become overgrown. Populations have declined by more than 90% since 1900.

children.name: Aphelocoma insularis
children.description: Found only on Santa Cruz Island, the largest of California's Channel Islands, the Island Scrub-Jay is larger, heavier-billed, and more deeply blue than its mainland relatives — classic island gigantism. With a total population of roughly 2,000 birds, it is among the most range-restricted birds in the United States. It is an important seed disperser of island oak and scrub oak, and has evolved distinct morphological and ecological traits in isolation.

children.name: Aphelocoma woodhouseii
children.description: The interior counterpart of the California Scrub-Jay, this species occupies piñon-juniper woodland, sagebrush, and scrubby oak of the Great Basin and Rocky Mountain region from Nevada east to Texas and south into Mexico. Named after the American naturalist Samuel Washington Woodhouse, it is duller blue and greyish below compared to coastal forms. A major disperser of piñon pine seeds, it is ecologically crucial to piñon-juniper woodland regeneration.

children.name: Cyanocorax
children.description: New World jays — a diverse genus of colourful jays ranging from Mexico to South America, with bold patterns of blue, black, white, and yellow. They inhabit a broad range of forest and woodland habitats and are typically gregarious, noisy birds that move in family parties. Many species are cooperative breeders, and all are intelligent omnivores that exploit a wide variety of food sources.

children.name: Cyanocorax yncas
children.description: One of the most brilliantly coloured corvids in the world, the Green Jay has emerald-green body, a bright yellow outer tail, blue crown, and black face and throat. It inhabits subtropical forest, thorn scrub, and woodland from southern Texas through Mexico and Central America to Peru and Bolivia. In Texas it is famous for using sticks as tools to dislodge prey from bark — one of the few wild corvids shown to use tools in North America.

children.name: Cyanocorax violaceus
children.description: The largest and most widespread jay of Amazonia, the Violaceous Jay is a deep purple-blue above with a black head, white-tipped tail, and a pale lavender-white underside. It inhabits the canopy and edges of tropical and gallery forest across the Amazon basin from Colombia and Venezuela south to Bolivia and Brazil. Noisy and conspicuous, it travels in groups of up to 12 birds, following army ant swarms to catch disturbed insects.

children.name: Cyanocorax cyanomelas
children.description: A large, conspicuous jay of open woodland, dry Chaco scrub, and forest edges in south-central South America, found from Brazil south through Bolivia, Paraguay, and Argentina. Mostly dark purplish-blue with a black head and throat, it travels in noisy parties and is a characteristic bird of the Pantanal and Chaco regions. It is a cooperative breeder, with subordinate group members assisting the dominant pair in raising young.

children.name: Cyanocorax mystacalis
children.description: One of the most distinctively patterned corvids, the White-tailed Jay has a brilliant cobalt-blue head with white nasal tufts and a white supercilium, black upperparts, and an entirely white tail and underparts. It is restricted to dry deciduous forest and scrub in a narrow zone of north-western Peru and south-western Ecuador. A poorly known species, it moves in small groups through thorn scrub and cactus country in the Tumbesian endemic bird area.

children.name: Cyanocorax cristatellus
children.description: Named for the distinctive curled or frizzled white crest feathers on the forehead, the Curl-crested Jay inhabits the cerrado (Brazilian savannah) woodland and gallery forest of central Brazil, Bolivia, and Paraguay. It is a conspicuous and noisy bird of forest-cerrado ecotones, travelling in boisterous groups. A cooperative breeder in which non-breeding helpers assist with nest defence and chick feeding.

children.name: Podoces
children.description: Ground jays — small to medium corvids of Central Asian deserts and semi-deserts, adapted for a largely terrestrial lifestyle with strong legs, a long decurved bill, and cryptic sandy plumage. They run rapidly across open ground to escape predators rather than taking flight, and forage by probing soil and digging for beetles, lizards, and seeds in some of the driest habitats on Earth.

children.name: Podoces panderi
children.description: A sandy-buff ground jay of the Kyzylkum and Karakum deserts of Uzbekistan, Turkmenistan, and Kazakhstan, named after the German-Russian naturalist Heinrich Pander. It is highly adapted to deep sandy desert, excavating long burrows for nesting in sand dunes and running swiftly between saxaul shrubs when disturbed. Its cryptic colouring makes it extremely difficult to spot against desert sand.

children.name: Podoces biddulphi
children.description: Confined to the Tarim Basin and adjacent deserts of Xinjiang, western China, this near-threatened ground jay inhabits saxaul desert and sandy plains with sparse scrub vegetation. Named after British army officer Michael Biddulph who led an 1873 mission through the region, it nests in cavities in clay banks or saxaul tree roots. It is one of the least-known corvids and is threatened by desertification and encroachment of agriculture into its desert habitat.
